topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips, heavy with the scent of aged ink. I traced the elegant script, the dates, the milestones—each entry a meticulously crafted piece of my existence, yet none of it felt mine. A cold, hollow ache settled in my chest, tightening my breath until it was a shallow, ragged thing. I reread the section detailing my early struggles, the triumphs, the quiet moments I thought were my own. Every sentence was a stranger's interpretation, a carefully curated narrative spun around my life. I dropped the document onto the mahogany desk; it landed with a soft, sickening thud. My hands began to tremble, a faint tremor that radiated up my arms, making it hard to steady myself. I stood up abruptly, needing air, but the room seemed to shrink around me. The silence in the study amplified the frantic drumming inside my own ears.

[story 2]

It happened during a routine archival search, a misplaced folder in the dusty university basement. What I found wasn't a record of my life; it was *my* life, rendered in a voice that was undeniably, chillingly, not mine. The words swam before my eyes, mocking the memories I held dear. A strange pressure built behind my sternum, a physical weight pressing down on my ribs. I sank onto the cold concrete floor, pulling my knees to my chest, trying to make myself small enough to disappear. Tears welled, hot and sudden, blurring the stark black lettering. When I finally looked up, my vision was blurry, and I could only see the oppressive texture of the concrete walls. A low, shaky sound escaped my throat—a sound that felt too small, too fragile for the magnitude of the deception unfolding.

[story 3]

The revelation hit me like a physical blow. I sat at the window overlooking the rain-slicked city, the glass mirroring the grey despair in my eyes. The biography lay spread across the sill, a cruel artifact. Reading the descriptions of my passions, the nuances of my character—they were perfectly accurate, yet utterly devoid of my lived experience. A profound emptiness washed over me, leaving a residue of nausea. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically scrub the words from my sight. A deep, shuddering exhale escaped me, a sound laced with utter defeat. I stood up slowly, my movements sluggish, as if wading through thick mud. The sheer unfairness of it made my jaw clench so tightly that a dull throb began behind my molars.
